summary: Paul Graham argues that Ron Conway’s success as an investor comes from genuinely being good, not just seeming good, because deals flow through trust and referrals. As the startup world has become more transparent and unpredictable, it’s increasingly hard to fake goodness, so the most successful investors tend to be the most upstanding.
key_points: Ron Conway’s benevolence directly drives access to top deals through referrals.
Transparency makes bad behavior visible and costly for investors.
Unpredictability makes strategic niceness impractical, favoring genuine goodness.
The startup world has shifted toward rewarding real integrity more than in the past.
money_quotes: It was by being benevolent that Ronco became so powerful.
In a sufficiently connected and unpredictable world, you can't seem good without being good.
The random college kid you talk to today might in a couple years be the CEO of the hottest startup in the Valley.
Though plenty of investors are jerks, there is a clear trend among them: the most successful investors are also the most upstanding.
As often happens, Ron discovered how to be the investor of the future by accident.
key_insight: In today’s startup ecosystem, real integrity is not just moral—it’s a competitive advantage that compounds through trust and referrals.
